title: Interpretable Energy Demand Forecasting for Mid-Sized University Buildings
research_area: Applied machine learning for sustainable campus operations
abstract: This demo thesis evaluates whether interpretable sequence models can forecast hourly electricity demand for mid-sized university buildings while producing explanations that facilities staff can use for operational decisions. The study uses a synthetic campus dataset with weather, calendar, occupancy, and building metadata. It compares gradient boosting, LSTM, and temporal fusion transformer baselines, then evaluates forecast accuracy and explanation usefulness across building types.
problem_statement: Campus energy teams need reliable short-term load forecasts to reduce peak demand and schedule equipment efficiently, but many high-performing forecasting models are difficult for non-technical facilities staff to inspect. Without clear explanations, staff may not trust model recommendations during abnormal weather, holidays, or occupancy changes.
research_gap: Prior building-load forecasting studies often optimize accuracy without testing whether model explanations align with operational knowledge. Fewer studies compare explanation stability across building types or examine whether interpretable outputs identify actionable drivers such as occupancy, cooling degree hours, and academic calendar effects.
objectives: Compare classical and neural forecasting models on a shared synthetic campus dataset; measure forecast accuracy across lecture, lab, library, and residential buildings; evaluate explanation consistency using SHAP and attention-based summaries; translate model findings into operational recommendations for facilities teams.
methodology_summary: The study uses 18 months of synthetic hourly electricity, weather, calendar, and occupancy data for 12 campus buildings. Data is split chronologically into training, validation, and test periods. Models include XGBoost-style gradient boosting, an LSTM sequence model, and a temporal fusion transformer. Evaluation uses MAE, RMSE, MAPE, peak-hour error, and explanation stability across rolling windows.
dataset_summary: The dataset is generated for demonstration only. It contains 157,680 hourly building records, simulated weather features, occupancy estimates, building type labels, and calendar indicators. No private campus data or personally identifiable information is included.
results_summary: The temporal fusion transformer achieved the lowest average MAE at 6.8 kWh, compared with 8.1 kWh for LSTM and 8.7 kWh for gradient boosting. Peak-hour error improved most for lecture halls and libraries. SHAP summaries consistently ranked temperature, occupancy proxy, hour of day, and exam-week indicators as the most important drivers, but explanation stability dropped during simulated holiday periods.
